goal: First-touch UX: next-step breadcrumbs in start/seal/status, soft-lint warnings inline at seal --submit, init no-git error guidance

summary.title: First-touch UX polish: breadcrumbs at start/seal + non-git init guidance
summary.what: Patches three rough edges identified in a first-touch friction audit ahead of tomorrow's user trial. (1) `mainline start` success appends a 3-line `Next:` block (append, git commit, seal --prepare) so users learn the loop on screen. (2) `mainline seal --submit` success now runs lint silently and prints a one-line summary when there are issues; conflicts are followed by an explicit `mainline check --prepare` pointer; ends with a `git push + PR` breadcrumb so users know the loop completes via GitHub. (3) `mainline init` in a non-git directory now carries SuggestedActions explaining `git init here` and the re-run command.
summary.why: Spec §11 sets the alpha bar at 'first-time users can complete the loop without reading AGENTS.md or --help'. The audit found 19 friction points; the top five all reduced to 'next-step is invisible after a successful command'. Tomorrow's trial users land on a fresh repo, run init, then have to guess what comes next. Putting the breadcrumb on screen at each transition is a strictly-additive change that keeps power-users unaffected (no flags, no defaults changed) and turns the first-touch path from 'discover by reading' into 'discover by running'.
summary.user_goal: A user who has never used Mainline can run the full init → start → seal → push loop tomorrow without leaving the terminal to read docs.

decisions.chose: Run lint silently after submit, print a one-line summary only when there are issues; never block.
decisions.rationale: Spec §9 Step 4 explicitly bans hard-blocking. Soft-remind in the seal output keeps the contract while making lint discoverable at the moment it pays off — the user just sealed; if the seal is poor, the next sealer (or future agent retrieval) inherits the rot. One-line summary preserves the existing seal output's cadence.

decisions.chose: Three numbered concrete commands: append, git commit, seal --prepare.
decisions.rationale: The spec is clear on the loop, but the loop is non-obvious unless you already know it. Three lines is the smallest set that communicates 'edit', 'commit', and 'seal' as distinct phases. Numbered list reads as sequence, not menu.

decisions.chose: Two SuggestedActions on the existing NOT_IN_GIT_REPO error.
decisions.rationale: The error code was the only signal; for a brand-new user it's an undecodable abbreviation. Two-line guidance covers both the empty-dir case (`git init`) and the wrong-cwd case (`cd to an existing repo`).

decisions.point: what NOT to change for tomorrow
decisions.chose: Skipped: README rewrite, ENV-var actor name, interactive prompts, lint-into-Daily-group taxonomy, doctor auto-suggestion on errors.
decisions.rationale: All real friction, but lower per-issue payoff than next-step breadcrumbs and higher behavioural-change cost. Each is a separate followup, not a tomorrow blocker.

rejected.alternative: Block submit on lint errors (full enforcement).
rejected.reason: Spec §9 Step 4 explicitly bans this. Hard-blocking would push frustrated agents to quote-checks-out-of-existence rather than fix the seal.

rejected.alternative: Add a `--no-next-steps` flag to suppress the breadcrumbs.
rejected.reason: Speculative complexity. Power users already have `--json` which produces no breadcrumbs. If the breadcrumbs annoy someone after tomorrow's trial we can add the flag then.

rejected.alternative: Print a longer wall-of-text quickstart on start success.
rejected.reason: Three numbered lines are the right depth — communicates the sequence without flooding the terminal. Anything more belongs in AGENTS.md.

rejected.alternative: Auto-run `mainline lint` inside the active draft via a hook.
rejected.reason: Drafts don't have structured summaries until seal time, so lint has no work to do until then. Wiring it earlier would just print noise.

risks: Adds output to commands users have already learned — long-time users may briefly experience 'why is there extra text?'. Acceptable: text is short, additive, and below the fold of the existing output.
Lint inside seal --submit calls Service.Lint which reads the view; on a slow disk or large view this adds ms-scale latency. Acceptable: fixed ceiling, never blocks, runs once per seal.

anti_patterns.why: Spec §9 Step 4 explicitly says hooks soft-remind, not hard-block. Same rule applies inside CLI subcommands.

anti_patterns.what: Adding next-step breadcrumbs to JSON output paths
anti_patterns.why: Scripts parse the JSON; an extra `next:` field would break consumers. Breadcrumbs live in the human-rendered branch only.

followups: README quickstart — Step 1 of spec roadmap is mostly done in the agent guidance template, but README's 5-minute quickstart still references the old next-step model.
Conflicts: surface CONFIDENCE so users know which ones to care about. Currently the score is shown but `low/medium/high` is mostly unread.
Doctor auto-suggestion: when init fails on a non-trivial path, hint at `mainline doctor --setup`.
ENV var support for actor name (`MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME`) so CI scripts don't have to thread the flag.
Optional: short interactive prompt at first init when --actor-name is missing AND stdin is a tty.

architectural_claims: Next-step breadcrumbs live in the CLI's human-rendered branch only; JSON outputs stay machine-parseable.
Lint inside seal --submit is soft-remind only — it never blocks. Hard enforcement remains a future opt-in hook.
Error SuggestedActions are the right surface for first-touch guidance on top of error codes; the code stays canonical, the actions teach.
behavioral_changes: `mainline seal --submit` text output: runs lint silently, prints 1-line summary if issues; on conflicts adds `mainline check --prepare --intent <id>` line; ends with git-push + PR breadcrumb.
`mainline init` in non-git dir: error includes 2 SuggestedActions explaining `git init` + re-run.
